text: So without any further ado, I'd like to welcome the co-founders of Z-List. Where is Jay Z? Yeah, kind of a Beyonce introduction. Are we good, Greg, on the sound? Okay. Okay, that's great. We could do that. But I was in Vermont and visited the Ben and Jerry's factory and heard the story of Ben and Jerry. I would just add that the economic state of the world at that time when we took that walk also really -- Yeah, because what year was that? That would be 2009. 2009. 10. We met in 2008. Yeah. There were not a whole lot of really exciting opportunities for folks like us with liberal arts backgrounds and who wanted to do something really cool and really great right now. So we made it for ourselves. Sure. Sure. So, Britta, why don't you answer this? So, what has been your biggest learning thing in the last three years? This, I just didn't think I would get through this and I learned X, Y, Z. What was -- can you name one thing that was really kind of a big deal for you? I hope you don't see this as a cop-out. And I think the biggest thing that I've learned is not to get attached to any one vision, to any one plan, but really to stay attached to the emotion behind it and not get too bogged down in the execution of, oh, it's day one, day 20. I am sure I will be right here. Because by day 20, it will be day one again. And that keeps it really exciting. And it took me a while to wrap my mind around that. But it's an interesting path, an interesting life that we have chosen. Not necessarily an easy one, but it is very interesting. Yeah. Yeah. Well, that's what startups are about, right? Yes. One of my tangible suggestions for anyone starting a business, I think especially women.
